title: Fattoush
subtitle: Lebanese bread salad with sumac dressing and toasted pitta
excerpt: A Levantine salad of romaine, cucumber, tomato, radish, spring onion, and herbs, dressed with olive oil, lemon, and sumac, with pieces of fried or toasted pitta baked into the salad at the end. The pitta softens slightly in the dressing but retains a crunch; the sumac gives the whole thing a sharp, fruity acidity.
sourceNotes: Fattoush is one of the classic Levantine bread salads, historically a way of using stale flatbread. It is documented in Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) and is a staple of Lebanese and Syrian home cooking. Sumac is the defining spice — its tartness replaces the vinegar used in Western bread salads.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare all the vegetables ahead but add the pitta and dress only just before serving.

content.text: Fattoush is built from leftovers: stale pitta, the vegetables that would otherwise wilt at the bottom of the fridge, and whatever soft herbs are to hand. In practice it is a sharper, more interesting salad than the ingredients suggest because of sumac — the ground dried berry that looks like paprika and tastes like concentrated lemon peel. It is sprinkled over the top and stirred through the dressing and it does more work than any single ingredient in most salads.

The pitta can be fried in oil for a richer result, or simply broken and toasted in a dry pan or oven. It should be added at the last moment, just as the salad is being served, so it has a chance to absorb a little dressing without going completely soft.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the torn {{pitta-bread}} pieces and fry, turning, for 3 to 4 minutes until golden and crisp on most sides. Drain on kitchen paper and season with a little salt.

Whisk the {{sumac}}, {{lemon}} juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, and a pinch of salt together in a small bowl. Taste — the dressing should be sharp and fruity. Combine the lettuce, tomatoes, cucumber, radish, spring onions, parsley, and mint in a large bowl. Pour over the dressing and toss. Add the fried pitta, toss once more, and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Pitta is soggy rather than crunchy
items.cause: Added too early or dressing applied too far in advance
items.fix: Add the pitta at the very last moment — it should be warm and crisp going into the salad and will soften slightly in about 2 minutes, which is the ideal texture

items.cause: Not enough sumac or the sumac is old
items.fix: Sumac loses potency with age; if yours smells faintly musty rather than sharp and fruity, it needs replacing. Add a second pinch just before serving

items.cause: Oil too hot or pieces too small
items.fix: Use medium rather than high heat, and keep the pieces large enough that they have some body. Very thin fragments burn quickly without crisping evenly
Dress the salad at the table or no more than a minute before serving — dressed leafy salads deteriorate quickly

content.text: Fattoush is a peasant dish of the Levant — originally a way of using stale flatbread so it would not be wasted. Like panzanella in Italy or panzanella's older cousins in the Arab world, it turns bread that has lost its appeal into the structural element of a salad. The sumac dressing is what makes it distinctly Middle Eastern: that combination of dried berry and lemon juice produces an acidity with a fruity undertone that Western vinaigrettes do not replicate. The word 'fattoush' likely derives from the Arabic 'fatt' (to crumble), referring to the broken bread.
